name: Kotiharjun Sauna
hours: Tue–Sun 14:00–21:30 (Wed women only 14:00–18:00, men from 18:00); closed Mon
highlights: Helsinki's last surviving traditional public wood-fired sauna, opened 1928. Cast-iron kiuas, no frills, old-school neighbourhood sauna culture — still run by the same family. Regulars come several times a week.

name: Sauna Arla
hours: Mon–Fri 14:00–21:00, Sat 12:00–19:00; closed Sun
highlights: A quietly beloved neighbourhood sauna in Punavuori, opened 1954. Small and unhurried, with a proper wood-fired kiuas and regulars who have been coming for decades. One of the few survivors in central Helsinki that remains genuinely non-touristic.

name: Sauna Hermanni
highlights: Waterfront wood-fired sauna on the Hermanni shoreline with a direct ladder into the Vanhankaupunginlahti bay. Run by a cooperative, volunteer-spirit ethos. Locals swim in the sea year-round, including through an avanto in winter.

name: Sompasauna
hours: Open 24/7, year-round (volunteer-run, self-service)
highlights: Helsinki's anarchist DIY sauna — a community-built, fully open, always-free waterfront sauna where anyone can come and throw wood on the fire. No staff, no rules beyond basic respect. Swimming directly into the harbour at any hour. A genuine social phenomenon and grassroots Helsinki institution.

name: Kulttuurisauna
hours: Wed–Sun 15:00–21:00 (hours vary by season); book online
highlights: Designed by architects Tuomas Toivonen and Nene Tsuboi, this wood-fired sauna opened in 2013 and quickly became an architectural icon. Floor-to-ceiling windows face Helsinki harbour; the sauna itself is a masterpiece of clean Finnish minimalism with a huge wood-fired kiuas. Mixed bathing is standard.

name: Lonna Sauna
address: Lonna island, Helsinki (ferry from Market Square / Kauppatori)
hours: Open Jun–Aug; ferry runs Thu–Sun; check visitsauna.com/lonna for schedule
highlights: A restored 19th-century military island fortress 15 minutes by ferry from central Helsinki. The wood-fired sauna perches on the rocky shore surrounded by sea — you sauna, then swim straight off the rocks. Combines historic architecture with pure classic Finnish sauna culture.

name: Löyly
highlights: Architecturally striking public sauna on the West Harbour shoreline, designed by Avanto Architects with a dramatic cascading timber facade. Two wood-fired saunas plus a smoke sauna; outdoor terraces and direct sea swimming. Internationally acclaimed, more design-hotel than neighbourhood sauna, but the saunas themselves are genuine and hot.

name: Allas Sea Pool
highlights: Floating pool and sauna complex right at the Market Square pier, with views across Helsinki harbour to the Cathedral. Three pools (seawater, heated, children's), rooftop sauna, and a restaurant. More resort than sauna-house, but the location on the water in the heart of the city is unbeatable for visitors.

name: Sipoonkorpi Underground Smoke Sauna
address: Sipoonkorpi National Park, Sipoo (trailhead: Kalkkiranta nature area)
hours: Open to groups by reservation; occasional public evenings
highlights: A traditional smoke sauna hand-carved partly into a rocky hillside in Sipoonkorpi National Park, east of Helsinki. Heated for hours until the stones glow and the smoke clears; participants then swim in a forest lake. One of the region's most atmospheric and authentic smoke sauna experiences within reach of the capital.

name: Rajaportti Sauna
highlights: Finland's oldest public sauna in continuous operation, opened 1906 on the iconic Pispala ridge. The smoke-stained wooden benches, original cast-iron kiuas, and the view over Lake Näsijärvi from the hill make this a pilgrimage site for any sauna enthusiast. Criminally cheap.

name: Rauhaniemi Folk Spa (Kansankylpylä)
hours: Mon 13:00–20:00 (women); Tue–Fri 13:00–21:00 (segregated); Sat–Sun 11:00–18:00
highlights: Municipal lakeside wood-fired sauna on Lake Näsijärvi, operating since the 1920s. One of Tampere's most beloved spots: steam until red, then plunge straight into the lake from the weathered docks. A raft sauna is moored offshore in summer. Utterly unpretentious and deeply Finnish.

name: Kaupinoja Sauna
hours: Varies by season; typically Tue–Sun afternoons; check city website
highlights: A less-visited municipal lakeshore sauna tucked into a small bay on Lake Näsijärvi. Locals rate it for its genuine heat and the calm, uncrowded atmosphere. Like Rauhaniemi but even quieter — the kind of sauna regulars want to keep to themselves.

name: Saunaravintola Kuuma
highlights: Harbour-front sauna and restaurant in Tampere's Laukontori warehouse district. A saunalautta (sauna raft) moors alongside; guests swim directly into the Tammerkoski canal. Known for excellent craft beer and herring, and a convivial post-sauna atmosphere beloved by Tampere locals.

name: Sauna Temple (Saunatemppeli)
hours: By reservation; occasional public evenings
highlights: A private sauna society operating in a converted industrial space in central Tampere. Focused on the meditative, ritualistic side of sauna: long, slow heating cycles, whisking with birch branches (vihta), and silence observed on the benches. Not for the casual visitor — a sauna philosopher's sauna.

name: Niemi-Kapee Smoke Sauna
address: Kapee, Teisko, Tampere municipality (Lake Näsijärvi north shore)
hours: Private rentals and group bookings; by appointment
highlights: A beautifully maintained traditional savusauna on a private lakeshore estate on Lake Näsijärvi north of Tampere. The sauna is heated for 4–6 hours before bathing; the löyly from the smokeless stones is legendarily soft and enveloping. Swimming off the dock into the lake completes the experience.

name: Forum Sauna
hours: Mon–Fri 13:00–21:00, Sat 11:00–18:00; closed Sun
highlights: Central Turku's traditional urban public sauna, opened in the 1950s, catering to the city's working-class sauna culture. No-frills tiled rooms, proper steam, a loyal clientele of weekly regulars. One of very few surviving inner-city public saunas in southwest Finland.

name: Villa Järvelä Smoke Sauna
hours: By reservation; private and group bookings
highlights: A 19th-century estate on the shores of Lake Littoinen with a meticulously maintained traditional savusauna. The smoke sauna is heated for six hours; bathing lasts well into the evening. The property also has a rowing boat for lake swims and a restored farmhouse for overnight stays. Considered one of the finest lakeside smoke sauna experiences in Southwest Finland.

name: Herrankukkaro Smoke Sauna
hours: Sauna evenings Fri–Sat in summer; book via website
highlights: An old farmstead on a small archipelago island with one of Finland's most celebrated smoke saunas. The sauna sits on a granite shore overlooking the Turku archipelago; after bathing, guests jump directly into the sea. Traditional Finnish dinner follows. Sauna From Finland certified. Internationally recommended by the Sauna Society of Finland.

name: Jätkänkämppä Smoke Sauna
hours: Thu 17:00–21:00 (public); other times by group booking
highlights: The world's largest smoke sauna in regular public use, seating up to 70 people at once. Built on the shore of Lake Kallavesi in a cluster of traditional log buildings. The weekly Thursday public evening is a Kuopio institution — after the long sauna session, everyone swims in the lake and eats grilled sausages. UNESCO savusauna heritage.

name: Kuopion Saana
hours: Tue–Sun 15:00–21:00; closed Mon
highlights: Kuopio's traditional harbour sauna, a wooden building right on the shore of Lake Kallavesi. After a properly hot wood-fired session, you step directly into the lake from the sauna dock. Unassumingly popular with locals and easily one of the best urban wood-fired sauna experiences in the lake district.

name: Väinölänniemi Winter Swimming Sauna
hours: Daily; changing rooms and sauna open year-round for winter swimming club members and public
highlights: The peninsula sauna used by Kuopio's avantouinti (ice swimming) community throughout winter. In season, 50 cm of ice are sawn open daily for the avanto plunge. The combination of electric sauna heat and an ice-cold Lake Kallavesi plunge at −25 °C is a quintessential Finnish winter experience.

name: Tupaswilla Smoke Sauna
hours: Fri–Sat evenings by reservation; occasional public sessions
highlights: Claims to house the world's largest smoke sauna, a log-built structure that can seat 120 people simultaneously on lakeside benches overlooking a Laukaa lake. The smoke sauna is UNESCO-certified heritage; the sheer scale of the experience — over 100 bathers sharing a single smoke chamber — is unlike anything else in Finland.

name: Sauna Village (Saunakylä)
hours: Open year-round; summer daily, winter weekends; check himosspa.fi
highlights: A unique outdoor sauna village with 24 different saunas in a forested lakeside setting — smoke saunas, barrel saunas, a sauna tent, a floating sauna, and more. Each sauna style is distinct; you wander between them throughout the day. The largest sauna variety in one location in Finland.

name: Sataman Viilu
hours: Tue–Sun 15:00–21:00; closed Mon
highlights: Jyväskylä's harbour-front wood-fired public sauna, right on the shore of Lake Jyväsjärvi. A proper no-frills sauna used daily by local residents; the dip into the lake is just steps from the sauna door. The Jyväskylä region is the Sauna Region of the World (2023 designation) and this is its workhorse public sauna.

name: Haikusauna
address: Kinkomaa, Muurame (17 km south of Jyväskylä)
hours: By reservation; occasional open evenings
highlights: A small, award-winning sauna studio on Lake Päijänne designed with Zen minimalism — inspired partly by Japanese onsen culture. Birch-panelled interior, meticulous wood-fired heating, and a private dock for lake swimming. Named after haiku poetry; silence and slowness are the ethos. Beloved on Finnish sauna forums as a rare meditative experience.

name: Kinkamon Pirtti Smoke Sauna
hours: By reservation; private group bookings
highlights: A historic farmstead smoke sauna on a lake in Keuruu, Central Finland. The sauna building dates to the 1800s and has never been electrified. The löyly from centuries-old stones is said to be uniquely dense and long-lasting. One of the most authentic surviving savusauna farms accessible to outside guests.

name: Revontuli Open Sauna Night
hours: Sauna nights: Fri–Sat evenings; check revontuli.com
highlights: A lakeside resort that runs hugely popular open sauna nights drawing sauna pilgrims from the Jyväskylä region. Multiple wood-fired saunas, a smoke sauna, a lakeside cooling pier, and bonfire. The open evenings are community events where strangers become sauna friends. In winter, the avanto is cut in the ice of a Päijänne bay.

name: Savutuvan Apaja Smoke Sauna
address: Haapaniementie 110, Kärkinen, near Jyväskylä (Lake Päijänne island)
hours: May–Sep: Thu–Sat evenings; winter by reservation
highlights: A remote island smoke sauna on Lake Päijänne, accessible only by boat in summer or across the ice in winter. The sauna is a restored 18th-century log cabin smoke sauna with a direct lake dock. Raw and magnificent — the sensation of extreme smoke sauna heat followed by Lake Päijänne cold is elemental.

name: Lampila Saunas
hours: Sat–Sun 12:00–19:00 (public); weekdays by group reservation
highlights: Municipal lakeside saunas at the Laajavuori recreation area, with direct access to a small forest lake. Multiple wood-fired cabins, no frills. Popular with families on summer weekends and winter swimmers in season. The Sauna Region of the World's most accessible municipal lakeside sauna.

name: Mallassauna
hours: Thu–Sat 15:00–21:00 in summer; check visitoulu.fi
highlights: A historic waterfront sauna on Pikisaari, Oulu's island neighbourhood once dominated by tar warehouses. The wood-fired sauna has a pier directly over the Oulu River estuary. After bathing, guests swim in the river current with views of the city bridge. A gem of northern Finnish sauna culture.

name: Koivurannan Saunalautta
hours: May–Sep; by reservation
highlights: A floating sauna raft on the Oulu River, moored in a quiet bend upstream from the city. The wood-fired sauna drifts gently in the current; guests cool off by jumping straight into the river. Popular with Oulu locals for summer celebrations — a quintessentially Finnish saunalautta experience far north of the Arctic circle's edge.

name: Kesän Sauna
address: Nallikari beach, Oulu (seasonal location near beach)
hours: Jun–Aug, daily afternoons; volunteer-run schedule varies
highlights: A volunteer-operated seasonal wood-fired beach sauna at Nallikari, one of the most popular sandy beaches in Northern Finland. The sauna runs on Bothnian Bay sea water for cooling. No charge, donations welcome — Oulu's answer to Helsinki's Sompasauna: grassroots, communal, and radically unpretentious.

name: Kiilopää Smoke Sauna (Kuurakaltio)
hours: Sauna evenings: Fri–Sat from 18:00; advance booking required
highlights: A classic smoke sauna in the Urho Kekkonen National Park fell area, where you heat, then roll in the snow or dip in a river-fed outdoor pool under the Northern Lights. At 300 m altitude on the subarctic fells, the post-sauna winter landscape is otherworldly. Run by the Finnish Trekking Association (Suomen Latu).

name: WNTR Sauna
address: Koskikatu area, Rovaniemi (Arctic Circle city centre waterfront)
hours: Daily; winter and summer; reservations via website
highlights: A sleekly designed wood-fired sauna right on the Kemijoki riverbank in Rovaniemi, just south of the Arctic Circle. Used as a base by the city's growing sauna and cold-water swimming community. In winter, guests cut an avanto in the river ice; in summer, the Midnight Sun sauna sessions run until 02:00.

name: Kakslauttanen Smoke Saunas
hours: Daily for resort guests; advance reservation required
highlights: Two large traditional smoke saunas at Finland's most famous Lapland resort, in the wilderness south of Saariselkä. The saunas seat 20–30; after bathing, guests roll in snow drifts or dip in a natural stream. Views of the Northern Lights from the cooling terrace in winter are extraordinary. The smoke sauna experience here is authentic despite the luxury resort setting.

name: Kontiolahti Avantouimarit Sauna
hours: Weekends in winter (ice swimming season); year-round by booking for groups
highlights: The ice-swimming and winter sauna club on Lake Höytiäinen, one of Finland's largest lakes. The club maintains an avanto throughout the frozen season and fires the wood sauna daily in winter. The lake is pristine, the sauna is the community's social hub, and the winter atmosphere is quietly magical.

name: Lehmonkärki AISTI Sauna Spa
hours: Thu–Sat evenings; Mon–Wed by reservation
highlights: A purpose-built premium smoke sauna on the edge of Lake Päijänne National Park. The AISTI experience is designed around all five senses: the smoke sauna is complemented by a cold lake plunge, a heated outdoor relaxation terrace, and locally foraged herbal teas. Repeatedly listed on Condé Nast Traveller's sauna recommendations.

name: Rauhaniemi Sauna Raft (Flou)
hours: Summer (Jun–Aug): daily 11:00–20:00; by reservation for groups
highlights: A floating wood-fired sauna raft moored at the Rauhaniemi beach, offering a sauna session directly on the lake surface. The raft is designed for groups; you heat, open the door, and step directly off the edge into Lake Näsijärvi. The perfect complement to the Rauhaniemi Folk Spa on shore.

name: Tampere Sauna Experience — Hermanni Saunalautta
hours: May–Sep: daily cruises 14:00, 17:00, 20:00
highlights: A saunalautta cruise on Lake Pyhäjärvi and the Tammerkoski canal waterway. The wood-fired sauna runs continuously as the raft motors through the Tampere waterways; guests can jump into the lake at anchor stops. Combines the best of Tampere's water city geography with a genuine sauna experience.

name: Sauna on the Rocks (Kallion Uimala)
hours: Summer: daily 13:00–20:00; occasional winter sessions
highlights: A city-operated wood-fired sauna perched directly on the Baltic shoreline rocks in the Kallio neighbourhood, Helsinki's most bohemian district. Steps lead straight into the sea from the sauna porch. Used daily by Kallio residents and the city's natural sea swimming community.

name: Vartiosaari Island Sauna
address: Vartiosaari island, Helsinki (ferry from Reposalmi, eastern Helsinki)
hours: Jul–Aug weekends; by reservation other times
highlights: A wood-fired sauna on a protected forested island in Helsinki's eastern archipelago. The island is car-free and the sauna sits on a rocky cliff above the sea. Swimming from the rocks in the open Baltic after an evening sauna, with the sunset over the Helsinki skyline to the west, is one of the city's most treasured summer experiences.

name: Lauttasaari Winter Swimming Sauna
hours: Mon–Fri 06:00–20:00, Sat–Sun 09:00–18:00 (winter swimming season Oct–Apr)
highlights: The sauna used by the Lauttasaari winter swimming club, one of the oldest and most active in Finland. The club cuts an avanto through the sea ice daily from November to March. At 05:30 on weekday mornings, a dedicated group of 50+ regulars gather before work for the sauna-avanto-sauna cycle — one of Helsinki's most committed sauna communities.

name: Sauna Boat M/S Tarjanne (Lake Cruise)
hours: Summer season; check visittampere.fi for schedule
highlights: A sauna on board a historic steam vessel plying the route between Tampere and Virrat across Lake Näsijärvi. The route covers 120 km of Finnish inland lake scenery. The sauna is wood-fired and traditionally fitted; passengers sauna in the afternoon and cool off in the lake at island stops.

name: Nokia Sauna Centre (Nokiansaari)
hours: Tue–Sun afternoons; smoke sauna Thu and Sat
highlights: A public sauna complex in Nokia, the industrial satellite city adjacent to Tampere, built on an island in the Nokianvirta river. Smoke sauna operates twice weekly; the river provides direct water access in summer and an avanto in winter. Nokia locals treat this as their primary weekly social institution.

name: Kyyhkylä Smoke Sauna
hours: Fri–Sat evenings (public); other times by reservation
highlights: An historic rehabiliation spa turned sauna resort on the shores of Lake Saimaa. The old smoke sauna is in a log building from the 19th century; after bathing, guests swim in the Saimaa lake system — the largest lake in Finland. The resort sits in the heart of the Finnish Lakeland.

name: Vuokalan Savusauna
hours: By reservation; Fri–Sat public sessions in summer
highlights: A preserved old-style savusauna on the waterways connecting Lakes Saimaa and Unnukka. Varkaus is at the centre of Finland's canal and lake country; the sauna sits right on a channel used by wooden boats. The combination of smoke sauna heat and the labyrinthine Saimaa lake system makes this one of the most geographically unique Finnish sauna experiences.

name: Hailuoto Island Sauna (Marjaniemi)
address: Marjaniemi lighthouse area, Hailuoto island (Bothnian Bay)
hours: Summer season; several operators run saunas near the lighthouse
highlights: A wood-fired sauna at the tip of Hailuoto, Finland's largest island in the Bothnian Bay, accessible by ferry from Oulu. The sauna sits on a sandy shore; after bathing, guests walk into the shallow warm Bothnian Bay. In winter, the island is encircled by sea ice as far as the eye can see. One of the most remote and atmospherically Nordic of all Finnish public saunas.

name: Kellari Sauna
highlights: A traditional basement (kellari) public sauna in central Pori — literally below street level. Pori's sauna culture is distinct from Tampere's or Helsinki's: quieter, more working-class, deeply embedded in the western coastal town's rhythms. The wood-fired kiuas in the low-ceilinged room produces an intensely enveloping heat.

name: Yyteri Beach Sauna
hours: Summer (Jun–Aug): daily 12:00–20:00
highlights: A sandy dune beach on the Bothnian Bay with a wood-fired sauna right on the shore. Yyteri is one of Finland's finest sandy beaches — rare in a country of rocky coasts. The sauna-then-sea experience here, with 2 km of white sand dunes, is unique in Finnish sauna geography.

name: Lappeenranta Harbour Sauna
hours: May–Sep daily 11:00–20:00; winter by arrangement
highlights: A wood-fired sauna on the Lappeenranta harbour shore, directly on Lake Saimaa. Lappeenranta is the gateway to the Saimaa lake system and the canal linking Finland to Russia — the sauna occupies a historic waterfront in this fortress town. After bathing, you swim in Finland's largest lake from a proper wooden dock.

name: Riverside Sauna — Arktikum Waterfront
address: Pohjoisranta, Rovaniemi (Ounasjoki riverbank near Arktikum museum)
hours: Jun–Aug: evenings by booking; Midnight Sun sauna nights Fri–Sat
highlights: A wood-fired riverside sauna on the Ounasjoki river bank in Rovaniemi, metres from the Arktikum Arctic Science Centre. In summer, the Midnight Sun means sauna at 01:00 in full daylight, followed by a swim in the Arctic river. In winter, this stretch of the river has natural ice swimming access with avanto cut by local clubs.

name: Sauna Pirtti
address: Haukkajärventie, Virrat (lake village 120 km north of Tampere)
hours: By reservation; summer weekends
highlights: A remote lakeside smoke sauna in the Virrat region — the northern end of the Lake Näsijärvi chain. Accessible by the historic M/S Tarjanne lake steamer or by road. The sauna is a restored pirtti (ancient Finnish one-room farmhouse) with the smoke sauna at one end and a lake dock at the other. A rare piece of living vernacular sauna architecture.

name: Joutseno Smoke Sauna
hours: Fri–Sat evenings; group bookings by arrangement
highlights: A working smoke sauna in Joutseno on the Saimaa shore, in a community that has kept the savusauna tradition alive without interruption. The bathing is communal in the old style: no time limits, no rushing. The Saimaa lake swimming is some of the cleanest freshwater in Europe.

name: Neitniemi Savusauna
hours: By reservation; Fri–Sat public evenings in summer
highlights: A traditional smoke sauna on Lake Vesijärvi near Lahti, easily reached by bicycle or kayak from the city. The smoke sauna is in a 19th-century log boathouse; the cooling dock extends over the lake. Lahti's connection of cycling and sauna culture — the Salpausselkä hills backdrop provides exceptional winter landscape for the post-sauna snow roll.

name: Sysmä Smoke Sauna
hours: Jun–Aug Fri evenings (public); other times by group booking
highlights: A village smoke sauna in Sysmä, one of the small lakeside communities on the east shore of Lake Päijänne. The sauna is run by the local village association and open to visitors on summer Fridays; it remains almost unknown outside the region. The löyly from the low-ceilinged smoke chamber is intense; the lake just outside the door is Päijänne — clear, cold, and immense.

name: Sauna Kelju
address: Pispala neighbourhood, Tampere (exact address varies — community sauna)
hours: Community sauna evenings; check local Tampere sauna groups
highlights: A community-run wood-fired sauna in the Pispala ridge neighbourhood — the same hillside as Rajaportti. Kelju means 'lovable rogue' and the sauna lives up to the name: informal, warm, mixed, and run entirely by volunteers. Sessions are announced on social media and draw the Tampere sauna underground.

name: Sauna Vaakuna
highlights: The main public sauna for Joensuu, capital of North Karelia, set on the Pielisjoki river bank. The wood-fired sauna has a riverside dock for swimming. Joensuu is the gateway to the Karelian forests and Orthodox sauna traditions that blend Finnish and Russian influences — this sauna retains that distinct eastern Finnish character.

name: Ylläs Smoke Sauna
hours: Winter season (Dec–Apr): Fri–Sat evenings; summer by arrangement
highlights: A smoke sauna at the foot of Ylläs fell — the largest ski resort in Finland. After a full day of skiing or snowshoeing on the fell, the smoke sauna provides the perfect recovery. In winter, guests roll in fell snow after bathing; the Northern Lights are a regular backdrop. Authentic Lapland savusauna in an extraordinary location.

name: Aavasaksa Fell Sauna
address: Aavasaksa fell, 95600 Ylitornio (Torne River valley, Lapland)
hours: Summer (Jun–Aug): daily; winter by arrangement
highlights: A wood-fired sauna on the summit area of Aavasaksa fell, the southernmost point in Finland where the Midnight Sun is visible. On Midsummer Night (Juhannus), hundreds gather on the fell for bonfire and sauna; on the longest night, you sauna at midnight in full sunlight, then walk to the summit for the most Finnish moment imaginable.

name: Koli National Park Smoke Sauna
hours: May–Oct: Fri–Sat evenings; winter by park arrangement
highlights: A smoke sauna in Koli National Park, on the shores of Lake Pielinen — the view from Ukko-Koli fell is the most famous landscape in Finnish art, inspiring Sibelius, Gallen-Kallela, and the national Romantic movement. After a smoke sauna session, guests swim in Lake Pielinen at the foot of the fell. A national sauna pilgrimage site.

name: Lohja Public Sauna (Aurlahti)
highlights: A municipal wood-fired sauna on the shores of Lake Lohjanjärvi, 60 km west of Helsinki. Lohja's lake culture is centuries old — the town grew around silver mines and lake trade. The sauna is a simple lakeside affair with wooden benches and a cold lake plunge; utterly authentic and overlooked by the Helsinki day-trippers who pass through on their way west.

name: Saunabussi Tampere (Sauna Bus)
hours: May–Sep: Fri–Sun afternoons and evenings
highlights: A wood-fired sauna installed inside a converted city bus. The Sauna Bus drives a route around Tampere's saunas and swimming spots, stopping for dips at Rauhaniemi, Kaupinoja, and the lakeshore parks. An absurdist and uniquely Tampere creation — the self-proclaimed sauna capital taking the joke to its logical extreme.

name: Tent Sauna on Lake Inari
address: Lake Inari, Inari municipality (accessed from Inari village, 69300 Inari)
hours: Year-round by arrangement; Northern Lights season Oct–Mar most popular
price: ~40–80 € per person (typically includes guide and transport)
highlights: A traditional Sami-influenced canvas tent sauna set up on the frozen surface of Lake Inari — the third-largest lake in Finland and the centre of Sami culture. The tent sauna (kota sauna) is heated with wood; guests then run across the snow and ice to the avanto cut in the lake. Under the Northern Lights. This is the most remote and elemental Finnish sauna experience available.

name: Floating Sauna Bus (Saunabus Helsinki)
hours: Apr–Oct: daily 12:00–22:00; groups and public sessions
highlights: A converted bus hull modified into a floating wood-fired sauna and social space, operating on Helsinki's sea and canal routes. The concept echoes Tampere's sauna bus but is fully maritime. Groups book the whole vessel; the sauna runs as the bus motors past Helsinki's islands. Equal parts sauna, boat trip, and performance art.

name: Kotka Maritime Sauna
hours: Jun–Aug: daily 12:00–20:00
highlights: A wood-fired sauna right on Kotka's Sapokanlahti waterfront, facing the Gulf of Finland archipelago. Kotka is Finland's maritime capital — historically the largest export harbour for Finnish timber, now a nautical heritage city. The sauna smell mixes with sea air and tar; you swim in the Baltic directly from the sauna dock.
